literal: the doctrine of non-one-sidedness; from an-eka-anta ("not of one end or aspect") + vāda ("doctrine")
invariant_definition: The Jaina doctrine that no existent is exhausted by a single predication or standpoint. Its metaphysical basis is the Jaina account of reality as simultaneously enduring and changing: a thing is a *dravya* with persistent identity, but it is also a stream of *paryāyas* or modes. Because predication tracks some aspect, relation, time, or practical standpoint, any true judgment is true under a determinate *naya* rather than as an unqualified total description. *Anekānta-vāda* therefore presupposes the paired apparatus of *naya* (standpoint-analysis) and *syād-vāda* (conditional predication). It is not the claim that contradictions are simply true together, nor the modern thesis that all opinions are equally valid. The doctrine instead blocks one-sided metaphysical absolutization: the Nyāya realist, Buddhist momentarist, and Vedāntin monist are each said to seize one feature of things and elevate it into the whole account. The load-bearing commitment is ontological and logical together: reality is many-sided, so assertion must mark the aspect under which it speaks.

per_school.definition: *Anekānta* rests on two classical claims: things are known through *pramāṇa* and *naya*, and whatever is real exhibits origination, cessation, and persistence together. The first claim blocks a single exhaustive viewpoint; the second gives the ontological ground for conditioned predication. A substance can be permanent as substance and changing as mode without contradiction, because the predicates are keyed to different respects.

Later Jaina logicians turn *anekānta* into a polemical method. Akalaṅka and Malliṣeṇa argue that rival schools err by absolutizing one admissible standpoint and denying the rest. The doctrine is thus not mere tolerance but a disciplined way of preserving determinate predication while refusing metaphysical one-sidedness. The seven *syād-vāda* forms are presented as the formal expression of this many-sided ontology.

Advaita treats *anekānta-vāda* as unstable. If opposite predicates are asserted without qualification, the view is self-contradictory; if they are asserted only under distinct standpoints, the doctrine reduces to the harmless point that language tracks context. Śaṅkara and later Advaitins therefore deny that Jaina many-sidedness yields a superior metaphysics; on their reading, it either abandons non-contradiction or says less than it appears to say.

Tattva-vāda reads *anekānta* as a failure of ontological determination. Madhva's realism requires stable and irreducible difference, not predicates whose force is continually redistributed across standpoints. From that perspective, the Jaina strategy weakens the reality of *bheda* by making it viewpoint-indexed rather than ontologically basic.

translator_note: "Non-absolutism" or "many-sidedness" is usually better than "relativism." The lazy gloss "relativism" imports modern epistemic permissiveness and suggests that contradictions are simply tolerated. Jaina usage is stricter: a predicate is valid only under a marked respect, and the metaphysical reason is the *dravya-paryāya* structure of the thing, not a modern sociology of viewpoints.
